title: Wave properties — frequency, wavelength, amplitude
examTip: v = fλ is the key equation — speed in m/s, frequency in Hz, wavelength in metres. Always check units before substituting.
introTips: Waves transfer energy from place to place without transferring matter — the medium itself just oscillates.
Transverse and longitudinal waves both carry energy, but they do it in very different ways.
The wave equation v = fλ connects three things you can measure — learn to rearrange it both ways.
Diffraction is why you can hear someone around a corner even though you can't see them.
Period and frequency are reciprocals: double the frequency and you halve the period.
intro: Every time you hear music, feel warmth from the sun, or send a text message, waves are doing the work. Waves are how energy moves through space — and understanding their properties is how engineers design everything from Wi-Fi networks to ultrasound scanners to noise-cancelling headphones. Whether a wave is a ripple on a pond, a sound wave from a speaker, or a light beam from your phone screen, the same handful of properties — frequency, wavelength, amplitude, and speed — describe them all. Get comfortable with these and you'll find the rest of the waves topic clicks into place.

keyPoints.heading: Transverse and longitudinal waves
keyPoints.content: All waves transfer energy without transferring matter. There are two types. Transverse waves: the oscillation (vibration) is perpendicular (at right angles) to the direction of travel — examples include light, all electromagnetic waves, water waves, and waves on a string. Longitudinal waves: the oscillation is parallel (along the same line) as the direction of travel, producing compressions (particles pushed together) and rarefactions (particles pulled apart) — sound is the key example. A useful memory check: transverse = 'T' shape, oscillation is 'T'-shaped (at right angles) to travel. Longitudinal = 'L' — oscillation is along the same line.
writeBullets: Transverse wave — oscillation perpendicular to direction of travel (e.g. light, water, EM waves)
Longitudinal wave — oscillation parallel to direction of travel (e.g. sound)
Compression — region where particles are pushed close together in a longitudinal wave
Rarefaction — region where particles are spread apart in a longitudinal wave
All waves transfer energy but not matter — the medium oscillates in place

keyTerms.def: A wave where oscillation is perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer — e.g. light, water waves
A wave where oscillation is parallel to the direction of energy transfer — e.g. sound
A region of high pressure in a longitudinal wave where particles are pushed together
A region of low pressure in a longitudinal wave where particles are spread apart

keyPoints.memTip: Transverse = T-shape (at right angles). Longitudinal = along the line. Sound is longitudinal; light is transverse.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say sound is transverse — stress the 'along the same line' definition and link it to the compression/rarefaction diagram.

keyPoints.heading: Amplitude, wavelength, frequency, and period
keyPoints.content: Amplitude (A): the maximum displacement of a point on the wave from its rest (equilibrium) position. Bigger amplitude = more energy carried by the wave. Measured in metres. Wavelength (λ): the distance between two successive points that are in phase (e.g. crest to crest, or trough to trough). Measured in metres. Frequency (f): the number of complete waves passing a point per second. Measured in hertz (Hz) — 1 Hz = 1 wave per second. Period (T): the time for one complete wave to pass a point. Measured in seconds (s). Period and frequency are reciprocals: T = 1/f and f = 1/T. Doubling frequency halves the period.
writeBullets: Amplitude — maximum displacement from rest position; bigger amplitude = more energy
Wavelength (λ) — distance from one crest to the next (or trough to trough); unit: metres
Frequency (f) — number of complete waves per second; unit: hertz (Hz)
Period (T) — time for one complete wave; unit: seconds (s)
T = 1/f — period and frequency are reciprocals of each other

keyTerms.def: Maximum displacement of a wave from its equilibrium (rest) position; larger amplitude means more energy
Distance between two successive points in phase, e.g. crest to crest; measured in metres
Number of complete wave cycles passing a point per second; measured in hertz (Hz)
Time for one complete wave cycle; T = 1/f; measured in seconds

keyPoints.memTip: AWFP: Amplitude, Wavelength, Frequency, Period — four properties, all linked by T = 1/f and v = fλ.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students regularly confuse amplitude with wavelength on a wave diagram — practise labelling diagrams and distinguishing vertical (amplitude) from horizontal (wavelength) measurements.

keyPoints.heading: The wave equation: v = fλ
keyPoints.content: The wave equation links the speed, frequency, and wavelength of any wave: v = fλ. Where v = wave speed (m/s), f = frequency (Hz), λ = wavelength (m). Rearrangements: f = v/λ and λ = v/f. Worked example 1: a wave has frequency 200 Hz and wavelength 1.5 m. Speed = 200 × 1.5 = 300 m/s. Worked example 2: a wave travels at 340 m/s with wavelength 0.17 m. Frequency = 340 ÷ 0.17 = 2000 Hz. Worked example 3: EM wave at 3×10⁸ m/s with frequency 1×10⁹ Hz. Wavelength = 3×10⁸ ÷ 1×10⁹ = 0.3 m. The wave equation applies to ALL wave types — sound, light, water, seismic.
writeBullets: Wave equation: v = fλ (speed = frequency × wavelength)
Rearrangement: f = v ÷ λ (frequency = speed ÷ wavelength)
Rearrangement: λ = v ÷ f (wavelength = speed ÷ frequency)
Units: v in m/s, f in Hz, λ in m — always check before substituting
Applies to all waves: sound, light, water, seismic waves

keyTerms.def: v = fλ; wave speed (m/s) = frequency (Hz) × wavelength (m)
How fast the wave travels through the medium; measured in m/s

keyPoints.memTip: v = fλ — 'very fast lambda': speed = frequency × wavelength. Cover what you want to find with your finger and multiply or divide the other two.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Insist students write the formula, substitute values with units, then calculate — method marks are available even if the final figure is wrong.

keyPoints.heading: Reflection, refraction, and diffraction
keyPoints.content: Reflection: waves bounce off a surface. The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection (both measured from the normal — a line perpendicular to the surface). The wave speed and frequency do not change. Refraction: when a wave crosses a boundary between two media, its speed changes, and if it hits at an angle, its direction changes too. A wave slowing down bends toward the normal; speeding up bends away from the normal. The frequency stays the same but wavelength changes. Diffraction: waves spread out as they pass through a gap or around an obstacle. Diffraction is most pronounced when the gap size is similar to the wavelength. A narrow gap diffracts more than a wide one (relative to wavelength). This is why long-wavelength radio waves diffract around hills, but short-wavelength light does not.
writeBullets: Reflection — waves bounce off a surface; angle of incidence = angle of reflection (from normal)
Refraction — wave speed changes at a boundary; direction changes if hitting at an angle
Slows down at boundary → bends toward normal; speeds up → bends away from normal
Diffraction — waves spread around obstacles or through gaps
Most diffraction when gap ≈ wavelength; wider gap = less diffraction (relative to wavelength)

keyTerms.def: Wave bounces off a surface; angle of incidence = angle of reflection, both from the normal
Change in wave speed at a boundary causing a change in direction (frequency unchanged)
Spreading of waves around an obstacle or through a gap; greatest when gap ≈ wavelength
An imaginary line perpendicular to a surface, used as the reference for measuring angles of incidence and reflection

keyPoints.memTip: Refraction: waves slow → bend toward normal (like a car wheel catching mud on one side). Diffraction is strongest when gap ≈ wavelength.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say diffraction happens 'when the gap is smaller than the wavelength' — correct phrasing is 'similar to the wavelength'; they should describe what happens to the shape of the wavefronts.

0.question: Sound waves are described as longitudinal. What does this mean?
options: The oscillation of particles is parallel to (along) the direction of wave travel
The oscillation of particles is perpendicular to the direction of wave travel
The wave can travel through a vacuum
The wave travels faster than light
0.correctFeedback: Correct! In a longitudinal wave, particles oscillate along the same direction as the wave travels, creating compressions and rarefactions.
0.wrongFeedback: Longitudinal means 'along the line' — particles vibrate parallel to the direction of travel. Transverse waves have oscillation perpendicular to travel (like light and water waves).

1.question: A wave has a frequency of 500 Hz and travels at 340 m/s. What is its wavelength?
1.correctFeedback: Correct! λ = v ÷ f = 340 ÷ 500 = 0.68 m. Always rearrange v = fλ to λ = v/f when you want the wavelength.
1.wrongFeedback: Use λ = v ÷ f = 340 ÷ 500 = 0.68 m. Make sure to divide speed by frequency — not multiply.

3.question: A wave passing through a gap diffracts (spreads out) most when:
options: The gap width is similar to the wavelength of the wave
The gap is much wider than the wavelength
The frequency of the wave is very high
The wave travels through a vacuum
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right! Diffraction is most pronounced when the gap size is approximately equal to the wavelength. A much wider gap produces much less spreading.
3.wrongFeedback: Diffraction is most pronounced when gap size ≈ wavelength. If the gap is much wider than the wavelength, the wave passes through with very little spreading.

commonMistakes: Confusing amplitude with wavelength on a wave diagram — amplitude is the vertical distance from rest to crest; wavelength is the horizontal distance from crest to crest.
Saying diffraction happens only when the gap is smaller than the wavelength — it is most pronounced when gap ≈ wavelength; it still occurs at other sizes but is less significant.
Forgetting to check units before using v = fλ — frequency must be in Hz and wavelength in metres to get speed in m/s.
Stating that refraction changes the frequency of a wave — refraction changes speed and wavelength, but frequency stays constant.
Describing sound as a transverse wave — sound is longitudinal; its particles oscillate parallel to the direction of travel.
examTips: v = fλ: always write the formula first, then substitute with units — method marks count even if you make an arithmetic slip.
For reflection and refraction questions, always measure angles from the normal, not from the surface itself.
When explaining diffraction, say 'the gap width is similar to the wavelength' — vague answers like 'the gap is small' will not get full marks.
In longitudinal vs transverse questions, give an example of each: sound (longitudinal), light/EM waves (transverse).
Period and frequency are linked: T = 1/f. If you know one, you can always find the other.
revisionCardBullets: Transverse wave — oscillation perpendicular to travel direction (e.g. light, EM waves, water)
Longitudinal wave — oscillation parallel to travel direction (e.g. sound); has compressions and rarefactions
Amplitude — maximum displacement from rest; bigger = more energy
Wavelength (λ) — crest to crest distance; unit: metres
Frequency (f) — waves per second; unit: Hz
Period (T) — time for one wave; T = 1/f; unit: seconds
Wave equation: v = fλ; rearranges to f = v/λ and λ = v/f
Reflection — angle of incidence = angle of reflection (from normal); speed unchanged
Refraction — speed changes at boundary; slows → bends toward normal
Diffraction — waves spread through gaps; most pronounced when gap ≈ wavelength
Frequency unchanged by reflection or refraction
